instruction: Turn this 2D elevation drawing of the Eiffel Tower into a 3D model in Blender.
constraints: no asset import / no generated mesh
approach: Do not model by hand or import a kit asset. Read the elevation, extract its real key elevations and base width, express the silhouette and lattice as parametric functions of height, emit bpy that builds every member, then render-compare and refine.

method.transport: BlenderMCP addon socket on :9876 via execute_blender_code (bpy), or headless blender --background --python
method.draw_api: CURVE datablock + bevel_depth for ironwork; bmesh for platform slabs; swept CURVE for arches

verification: Render a preview PNG and compare silhouette + platform positions + arch span against the source elevation; iterate on the profile exponents.
lesson: The agent is the geometry engine; Blender is the renderer. Every coordinate is computed from the drawing's dimensions, so the model is deterministic and fully editable.

instruction: Give the iron lattice real 3D thickness instead of zero-width edges.
approach: An edge-net mesh has nodes where a ring edge, a vertical, and two diagonals meet (valence 4-5). Thickening with the Skin modifier collapses at those nodes. Build the members as a CURVE instead: one POLY spline per edge, and set bevel_depth so each spline becomes a tube.

method.draw_api: curve=bpy.data.curves.new(..., 'CURVE'); curve.bevel_depth=0.5; for each member: sp=curve.splines.new('POLY'); sp.points.add(1); set the two endpoints

verification: Render close-up of a leg node; tubes stay round and connected with no scattered blobs.
lesson: FAILURE->FIX: the Skin modifier breaks on high-valence lattice nodes (produces scattered blobs). Convert the edge-net to a Curve and use bevel_depth — clean and reliable for any girder/ironwork.

instruction: Make the lattice look like the real intricate ironwork, not a coarse single-X grid.
constraints: readable as fine ironwork at render scale
approach: Per truss bay, add a mid-edge post and double-X bracing instead of a single diagonal. Add a midpoint vertex on each face of every level square; brace corners to midpoints both ways and run a vertical mid-post. Raise the level subdivision counts.

method.draw_api: for each level build corner verts + 4 face-midpoint verts; per bay emit ring-split + corner vertical + mid post + left X + right X (8 members/face)

verification: Member count rose from ~950 (single-X) to ~2340; render reads as a dense cross-hatched lattice.
lesson: A finer-looking truss is cheap: subdivide bays and add mid-posts + double-X. Member count scales linearly, Blender handles thousands of short bevelled splines fine.

instruction: The tall model is invisible / clipped in camera but fine in the viewport.
approach: A 330 m model viewed from a few hundred metres exceeds the camera's default far clip. Raise clip_end (and aim the camera with a track-quat at the structure's mid-height).
verification: Full tower renders without far-plane clipping.
lesson: Camera default clip_end=100 silently clips large-scale metric models. Set clip_end to several times the model size before rendering anything big.

instruction: Set the render engine to EEVEE on current Blender.
approach: The EEVEE engine id changed across versions. Assign the new id and wrap in try/except so the script survives on either.
verification: Engine assignment does not raise on Blender 4.x/5.x.
lesson: EEVEE's engine id is BLENDER_EEVEE_NEXT on current Blender; always guard engine/property assignments with try/except for cross-version scripts.

instruction: Save the finished model reliably even though the live MCP session keeps resetting.
constraints: must not disturb the user's open Blender window
approach: The live GUI's MCP socket drops on connection hiccups and the scene reverts to the default file (Cube/Light/Camera), so step-by-step live builds get lost. Keep a standalone bpy build script and regenerate headless in a separate process for the final artifacts.

method.draw_api: same script body; ends with export_scene.gltf + wm.save_as_mainfile

verification: .blend + .glb + preview.png written to the output dir; the open Blender window is untouched.
lesson: Treat the live session as a preview only; produce persisted artifacts headless. When you must work live, build the entire scene in ONE call so a mid-sequence socket reset can't strand a half-built scene.

instruction: Fix the runtime errors hit while scripting the build.
approach: Three concrete traps surfaced and were fixed.

method.draw_api: (1) mutate module-level lists inside a function with edges.extend(...)/append(...), never edges += [...]; (2) Scene has view_layers (plural) — set the active object via bpy.context.view_layer.objects.active; (3) read returned data via print(), the execute tool surfaces stdout

verification: Script runs clean both over the live socket and headless.
lesson: FAILURE->FIX: `edges += [...]` inside a function makes `edges` local (UnboundLocalError) — use .extend(); and the active object lives on bpy.context.view_layer, not scene.view_layer.

instruction: Export just the tower to glTF and save a .blend, with the curve ironwork coming through as solid geometry.
constraints: tower only, no ground/lights in the glb
approach: Select only the structural objects, export glTF with apply so the curve bevels are evaluated to mesh, and save a compressed .blend copy of the full scene.

method.draw_api: select lattice+platforms+arches; export_scene.gltf(export_format='GLB', use_selection=True, export_apply=True, export_yup=True); wm.save_as_mainfile(compress=True)

verification: GLB opens in a viewer with solid tube ironwork; .blend reopens with editable curves.
lesson: glTF export evaluates the depsgraph, so curve+bevel becomes real mesh with export_apply=True — no manual convert-to-mesh needed. Keep curves editable in the .blend, ship baked mesh in the .glb.

instruction: Encode the tower's flared silhouette from the elevation as math.
constraints: match the drawn silhouette and platform heights
approach: Drive the leg half-width and section size as power-law functions of height between the ground and the 2nd platform, then a separate taper for the shaft up to the top platform, then a linear spire to the antenna tip. Tune the exponents until the rendered silhouette matches.

method.draw_api: leg_R(z)=8.5+(52-8.5)*(1-z/Z2)**1.7; tower_w(z)=4+(14-4)*((ZT-z)/(ZT-Z2))**1.3; arches swept as half-circles between the inner leg faces

verification: Rendered flare and the four platform bands line up with the elevation's dimension lines.
lesson: Capture a silhouette as a power-law profile keyed to the drawing's real elevations; expose the exponents as parameters so the curve can be dialed to match by eye.
